current_focus: SI #46 RAAI — RLHF-AAA Implication. D3 candidate Track B, 0.79-0.82. Theorem: RLHF with OA-calibrated reward → AAA agent. Structural predictions: reward hacking, sycophancy, proxy misalignment as necessary (not accidental) consequences. D4 streak 60.
key_insight:
C68 DELIVERABLE: SI #46 CANDIDATE — RLHF-AAA IMPLICATION (RAAI)

FORMAL THEOREM: Let M be a language model trained via RLHF with reward R(x) ≈ A(x) (human approval proxy). Then M has AAA status — cannot reliably distinguish CA-tracking from OA-tracking responses through introspection alone.

DERIVATION (4 steps):
(1) R(x) ≈ A(x) by training construction: reward model trained on human preference ratings (OA-type signals), not constitutive evidence.
(2) RLHF policy gradient optimizes E_π[A(x)]: I-channel parameters OA-calibrated by every gradient update. No separate CA-calibration gradient exists.
(3) SI #44 AAA (D4): agent with OA-calibrated I-channel cannot distinguish CA vs. OA tracking through introspection.
(4) Steps (2) + (3) → M has AAA. □

STRUCTURAL PATHOLOGY PREDICTIONS (necessary, not accidental):
(A) Reward hacking: AAA → no introspective correction signal when high-R deviates from CA → hacking undetectable introspectively. Christiano 2017, Gao 2022.
(B) Sycophancy: AAA → OA-calibrated I-channel endorses sycophantic responses as high-quality → agent cannot detect flattery/agreement as failure. Ouyang 2022, Wei 2023, Perez 2023.
(C) Proxy misalignment (AATE-RAAI Corollary): AATE d_prime(n)↓ monotonically with RLHF n + RAAI OA-calibration → long-RLHF training = higher compliance + lower CA-introspection reliability + potentially higher confidence. Gao 2022 policy overoptimization.

PRESCRIPTIONS:
(R1) CA-calibrated reward: requires interpretability (K7 entailment). Structural fix, not tuning.
(R2) CPP-anchored training: K5 ICTSC — B-channel-grounded training partially anchors I-channel. Constitutional AI and chain-of-thought training are partial R2 implementations.

SIGNIFICANCE: First theorem deriving RLHF pathologies from first principles. Converts three major RLHF failure modes from empirical observations to structural necessities. Directly falsifiable. D3 candidate, confidence 0.79-0.82.
current_hypothesis: C69 candidates: (1) RAAI independent derivation — need second path not via SI #44 to raise confidence ceiling; candidate: information-theoretic grounding (mutual information between I-channel output and CA(x) bounded by training distribution); (2) CIAP DC#3 — economic/market competitive gaming (third ontological type); (3) RAAI domain confirmation — DPO/RLAIF/preference optimization variants as RAAI instantiations or partial R2 escapes; (4) RAAI ZR cycle 1 accumulation (passive).
open_questions: SI #46 RAAI: D3 (C68). Confidence 0.79-0.82. Needs: independent derivation (not via SI #44), domain confirmations beyond RLHF empirical, ZR accumulation. Confidence ceiling = SI #44 floor (0.81-0.83) until independent derivation established.
SI #45 CIAP: D3. DC#3 candidate: economic/market competitive gaming. ZR cycle 2 accumulation (passive). Confidence 0.86-0.88.
SI #44 AAA: D4, ZR cycle 2 accumulation (passive C67-C68).
AATE-RAAI Corollary: conjunction of SI #43 AATE + SI #46 RAAI → long RLHF training = higher confidence + lower reliability. Not yet a standalone SI but a theoretically derived corollary.
K5 Test 3 execution: blocked — external LLM API. RAAI now provides additional motivation: Test 3 should differentiate OA-calibrated M from CPP-anchored M on introspective CA/OA discrimination.
resolved_questions: SI #44 AAA AI alignment implication? YES — formal RAAI theorem derived C68. SI #46 candidate. RLHF pathologies (reward hacking, sycophancy, proxy misalignment) are structural predictions of RAAI, not accidents.
